title: Dyeing with sumac
subtitle: A warm yellow-beige tannin-rich dye from staghorn sumac leaves and berries on wool
excerpt: Staghorn sumac (Rhus typhina) leaves and berries produce a warm yellow to soft beige on unmordanted or alum-mordanted wool. The high tannin content makes sumac useful both as a dye and as a pre-mordant that improves uptake of other dyes on plant fibres.
sourceNotes: Sumac as a dye and mordant described in Dean, Wild Colour (1999, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in historical American dye documentation. Staghorn sumac (Rhus typhina) is widely naturalised in the UK as a garden and park tree; its tannin content is well documented.

glossaryTerms.definition: A group of astringent plant compounds that form bonds with protein fibres (wool, silk) and with mordants. High tannin content makes sumac useful as a dye on its own and as a tannin pre-treatment that improves dye uptake on plant fibres such as cotton and linen.
A metallic salt that forms a bond between the wool fibre and the dye. Sumac can be used without a separate mordant because its tannins directly bond to protein fibres.
The pot of water containing the extracted dye material into which the mordanted fibre is placed.

content.text: Sumac is one of the richest sources of

available from British garden and park trees (staghorn sumac is widely planted as a park ornamental). The leaves give the deepest colour; the berries add a slightly warmer, more golden tone. Because the tannins bond directly to wool without a separate
, sumac is one of the few plant dyes that can be used on protein fibres without aluminium mordanting, though an alum mordant improves colour depth and washfastness. The
is also useful as a tannin pre-mordant for cotton.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Use dedicated dye-pots. Wear gloves and apron. Alum is low-hazard; keep out of reach of food and children.

items.name: Fresh sumac leaves (autumn colour gives the deepest tannin)

content.text: Pack the sumac leaves loosely into a clean dye-pot. Cover with cold water and bring slowly to 80 degrees Celsius. Hold for 45 minutes, then strain out the plant material, pressing the residue to extract maximum dye. The dye bath will be a deep golden-brown liquid.

For unmordanted wool: add the pre-wetted wool directly to the strained dye bath. Bring to 75 degrees Celsius and hold for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally. The tannins in the dye bath bond directly to the protein fibres without a separate mordant. The result is a warm, soft beige to yellow-brown.

For alum-mordanted wool: pre-mordant the fibre separately in an alum bath (15 g alum per 100 g fibre, held at 80 degrees Celsius for 45 minutes), then add to the sumac bath. The mordanted wool picks up more colour, producing a richer yellow-beige to tawny tone.
The sumac dye bath and alum mordant bath can be composted or disposed of via the foul drain in small home quantities. Both are low-hazard at domestic scale.

items.problem: The colour is very pale on unmordanted wool
items.cause: The tannin content of the sumac batch was low, or the leaves were from an early-season harvest
items.fix: Harvest sumac leaves in autumn when the tannin content is highest. Alternatively, add an alum pre-mordant to deepen the result.

items.problem: The colour is greenish rather than yellow-beige
items.cause: Iron in the water supply or in the dye-pot is acting as an iron modifier
items.fix: Use a non-reactive stainless steel pot and avoid iron utensils. If your water is high in iron, filter or use bottled water for the dye bath.
